title: AI Governance for PMs
description: Learn to build governance frameworks that enable responsible AI deployment — from risk assessment to compliance requirements to ethical considerations.
learningObjectives: Design an AI governance framework appropriate for your organization's size, industry, and risk tolerance
Build an AI review process that catches critical issues without becoming a bureaucratic bottleneck
Navigate data privacy, compliance, and bias considerations when planning AI features

sections.title: AI Governance Fundamentals for PMs
sections.content:
AI governance sounds like something for legal and compliance teams, not product managers. In reality, PMs are the most important people in AI governance because they make the day-to-day decisions about what AI features get built, how they behave, and what safeguards are in place.

**What AI Governance Actually Means**

AI governance is the set of policies, processes, and practices that ensure AI features are developed and deployed responsibly. For a PM, this translates into concrete decisions:

• **What data flows through the AI system?** Every piece of user data sent to Claude is a governance decision. What gets included in the prompt, what gets stored, and what gets discarded are product decisions with governance implications.
• **What can the AI do and not do?** The boundaries you set in system prompts, the topics you allow or disallow, and the actions you permit the AI to take on behalf of users are all governance decisions.
• **Who reviews AI outputs before they reach users?** Whether outputs go directly to users or pass through human review is both a product design and governance decision.
• **How do you handle failures?** Your incident response plan for AI errors — who gets notified, how quickly you respond, what you communicate to affected users — is a governance process.

**Why PMs Should Care**

PMs who treat governance as an afterthought face predictable consequences:

• **Delayed launches.** Legal and compliance reviews that happen at the end of a project routinely add 2-4 weeks to timelines. Starting governance conversations early avoids last-minute surprises.
• **Reputational damage.** An AI feature that produces biased, offensive, or incorrect outputs generates headlines and erodes user trust. Governance prevents these outcomes before they happen.
• **Regulatory exposure.** Regulators in the EU, US, and other markets are actively developing AI-specific regulations. Features built without governance in mind may need expensive retrofitting to comply.
• **Internal credibility.** PMs who proactively address governance concerns earn trust from legal, security, and leadership teams. This translates into faster approvals for future AI initiatives.

**The Governance Maturity Spectrum**

Not every organization needs the same level of governance. Match your approach to your context:

• **Level 1: Ad Hoc** — AI features are reviewed case-by-case. Appropriate for early-stage startups shipping their first AI feature. The PM and engineering lead make governance decisions together.
• **Level 2: Documented** — Your organization has written guidelines for AI features, including a checklist PMs complete before launch. Appropriate for growing companies with multiple AI features in development.
• **Level 3: Systematic** — An AI review board evaluates all AI features against established criteria. Data privacy impact assessments are mandatory. Appropriate for companies in regulated industries or with significant AI exposure.
• **Level 4: Proactive** — Dedicated AI ethics resources, red-teaming exercises, ongoing monitoring of deployed AI features, and regular governance audits. Appropriate for companies where AI is core to the product and errors have high stakes.

Most companies should aim for Level 2 initially and progress to Level 3 as their AI portfolio grows.

sections.title: Building an AI Review Process
sections.content:
An AI review process ensures that AI features are evaluated for risk, quality, and compliance before they reach users. The challenge is building a process that catches real issues without becoming a bureaucratic bottleneck that slows down every AI initiative.

**The AI Feature Review Checklist**

Every AI feature should answer these questions before launch. The depth of each answer scales with the feature's risk level:

• **Data inventory:** What user data does this feature send to Claude? Is the data classified (PII, financial, health)? Is the data handling consistent with your privacy policy and user consent?
• **Output controls:** What guardrails prevent Claude from producing harmful, offensive, or misleading content? Have you tested adversarial inputs? Are system prompt boundaries clearly defined?
• **Human oversight:** At what point does a human review Claude's output? Is the human-in-the-loop mechanism effective, or is it a rubber stamp?
• **Failure handling:** What happens when Claude produces a bad output? How is the error detected, logged, and resolved? Who is notified?
• **Monitoring plan:** How will you track output quality after launch? What metrics trigger an alert? Who is on call for AI-related incidents?
• **User transparency:** Does the user know they're interacting with AI? Can they opt out? Can they report issues?

**Risk-Tiered Review**

Not every AI feature needs the same level of review. Classify features into risk tiers and scale your process accordingly:

• **Low risk:** Internal tools, non-customer-facing features, or features where the AI assists but doesn't decide. Example: Claude suggesting tags for internal documents. Review: PM + engineering lead sign-off.
• **Medium risk:** Customer-facing features where the AI generates content that users see, but human review is built in. Example: Claude drafting customer emails that agents review before sending. Review: PM + engineering lead + legal review of customer-facing language.
• **High risk:** Features where AI outputs directly affect decisions about people, money, or safety. Example: Claude scoring insurance claims or prioritizing support tickets by severity. Review: Full AI review board including PM, engineering, legal, data science, and a domain expert.

**Avoiding Review Bottlenecks**

The biggest risk of a governance process is that it becomes a gate that blocks all AI development. Prevent this by:

• **Making low-risk reviews self-serve.** Create a checklist that PMs can complete independently for low-risk features. Only escalate to formal review when the checklist flags a concern.
• **Running reviews in parallel with development.** Don't wait until the feature is built to start the review. Begin the governance conversation during the planning phase so that issues are identified early.
• **Setting SLAs for reviews.** Legal and compliance teams should commit to review timelines. A governance review that takes 6 weeks with no visibility into progress is broken.
• **Documenting precedents.** When a review board makes a decision, document the reasoning. Future features with similar profiles can reference the precedent instead of starting from scratch.

sections.title: Data Privacy and Compliance
sections.content:
Every AI feature has a data story — what data goes in, what comes out, and where it flows in between. As a PM, you don't need to be a privacy lawyer, but you need to understand the basics well enough to make informed decisions and ask the right questions.

**Data Classification for AI Features**

Before building any AI feature, classify the data it will process:

• **Public data** — Information that's already publicly available. Using public data with Claude carries the lowest risk. Example: summarizing published product documentation.
• **Internal data** — Company data that isn't publicly available but doesn't contain personal information. Example: analyzing internal sales reports. Risk: competitive sensitivity and potential data leakage.
• **Personal data (PII)** — Any data that identifies or could identify a specific individual. Names, email addresses, phone numbers, user IDs. This triggers privacy regulation requirements.
• **Sensitive personal data** — Health information, financial data, biometric data, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, sexual orientation. Processing this with AI requires the highest level of governance and often explicit user consent.

**Key Regulatory Frameworks**

You don't need to memorize regulations, but you need to know which ones might apply to your AI feature:

• **GDPR (EU/EEA)** — If you process data of EU residents, GDPR applies. Key requirements: lawful basis for processing, data minimization (only send Claude what it needs), right to explanation (users can ask how automated decisions were made), and data processing agreements with API providers.
• **CCPA/CPRA (California)** — Similar to GDPR but with specific requirements around sale and sharing of personal information. If Claude processes data that's later used for personalization or advertising, CCPA may apply.
• **SOC 2** — Not a regulation but a trust standard many B2B customers require. If your customers ask for SOC 2 compliance, your AI data flows are in scope. Document what data goes to Claude and how it's handled.
• **Industry-specific regulations** — HIPAA (healthcare), FERPA (education), FINRA/SEC (financial services), PCI DSS (payment data). If your product touches these industries, AI data flows need to comply with industry-specific rules.

**Practical Privacy Decisions for PMs**

These are the privacy decisions you'll face most often when building AI features:

• **What to include in prompts.** Send Claude the minimum data necessary to produce a good output. If you're summarizing a support ticket, do you need the customer's name and email, or can you anonymize them? Minimizing data reduces risk.
• **Data retention.** Does your application store Claude's responses? For how long? Can users request deletion? Build data lifecycle management into your feature design from the start.
• **User consent.** Does your existing privacy policy cover sending user data to an AI API? If not, you may need to update it and obtain new consent. This is a common blocker that PMs discover too late.
• **Cross-border data transfer.** If your users are in the EU and Claude's API processes data in the US, you may need additional data protection safeguards. Check with legal early.
• **Audit trail.** For regulated industries, you may need to log what data was sent to Claude, what was returned, and what action was taken. Design this logging into the feature architecture, not as an afterthought.

sections.title: Bias Detection and Mitigation
sections.content:
AI bias is not a theoretical concern — it's a practical product risk. Claude, like all language models, can produce outputs that reflect or amplify biases present in its training data. As a PM, you need to understand where bias can appear, how to detect it, and what to do about it.

**Where Bias Appears in AI Features**

Bias can enter your AI feature at multiple points:

• **Input bias** — The data you send to Claude may itself be biased. If your customer support training data over-represents certain demographics, Claude's responses may be subtly optimized for those groups.
• **Prompt bias** — The examples, instructions, and framing in your prompts can introduce bias. If your few-shot examples all feature male professionals in technical roles, Claude may default to that framing in its outputs.
• **Output bias** — Even with neutral inputs and prompts, Claude may produce outputs that treat different groups differently. This can manifest as different tones, different levels of formality, or different assumptions about users based on names or other identity signals.
• **Evaluation bias** — Your quality evaluation process may be biased. If your test set doesn't include diverse inputs, you won't catch biased outputs during testing.

**Detecting Bias**

Bias detection for AI features doesn't require a PhD in machine learning. It requires systematic testing with diverse inputs:

• **Demographic parity testing** — Run the same query with different names, locations, or demographic signals and compare outputs. Does Claude's product recommendation change when the user's name changes? Does the tone of a support response differ based on implied gender?
• **Outcome distribution analysis** — If your AI feature makes decisions (prioritization, categorization, scoring), analyze the distribution of outcomes across demographic groups. Are certain groups consistently scored lower, categorized differently, or deprioritized?
• **Red-teaming for bias** — Dedicate specific testing sessions to probing for bias. Have team members from diverse backgrounds interact with the feature and report instances where outputs feel biased, unfair, or uncomfortable.
• **User feedback analysis** — After launch, segment user satisfaction data by demographics (where available and appropriate). Disparities in satisfaction may indicate bias in the AI feature's behavior.

**Mitigating Bias**

Once you've identified bias, you have several tools to address it:

• **Prompt engineering** — Explicitly instruct Claude to treat all users equally, avoid assumptions based on names or demographics, and use neutral language. Include diverse examples in few-shot prompts.
• **Input preprocessing** — Remove or anonymize demographic signals from the data sent to Claude when those signals aren't relevant to the task. If Claude doesn't need the user's name to generate a report, don't include it.
• **Output post-processing** — Add a review step that checks outputs for biased language or disparate treatment before they reach users. This can be automated for certain types of bias (gendered language, for example) and manual for subtler cases.
• **Diverse evaluation** — Build an evaluation set that includes inputs from diverse demographic groups, geographies, and use cases. Test against this set with every prompt change.
• **Ongoing monitoring** — Bias can emerge over time as usage patterns change. Set up monitoring that tracks output distributions and flags anomalies for review.

**The Fairness vs. Performance Tradeoff**

Sometimes bias mitigation affects output quality. Removing a user's name from the prompt might reduce personalization quality. Using strictly neutral language might make outputs feel generic. These are real tradeoffs that PMs need to make deliberately, not accidentally. Document your decisions and the reasoning behind them.

sections.title: Design an AI Governance Checklist
sections.content: Build a practical governance checklist that a PM at your organization could use to evaluate any AI feature before launch.

exercise.prompt:
You're the PM responsible for establishing AI governance practices at a mid-stage B2B SaaS company (200 employees, Series C, 500 enterprise customers). The company is launching its first three AI features this quarter and currently has no AI governance framework. Leadership has asked you to create a practical governance checklist.

Design an AI governance checklist that covers:

1. **Risk Classification** — Define 3 risk tiers (low, medium, high) with specific criteria for classifying AI features into each tier. Include at least 2 concrete examples of features that would fall into each tier.
2. **Pre-Launch Checklist** — Create a checklist of 8-10 items that must be completed before any AI feature launches. Each item should be specific and actionable (not vague like "consider privacy"). For each item, note which risk tiers require it.
3. **Data Privacy Section** — Identify the 5 most important privacy questions a PM should answer for any AI feature. For each question, explain why it matters and what a good answer looks like.
4. **Bias Assessment** — Describe a practical bias testing protocol that a small team (no dedicated ML ethics team) can execute in 2-3 days. Include specific test types, sample sizes, and decision criteria.
5. **Post-Launch Monitoring** — Define what ongoing monitoring looks like after an AI feature ships. What metrics do you track, how often, and what triggers an escalation?
hints: Risk tiers should be based on factors like: Does the AI output reach customers directly? Does it influence decisions about people? Does it process sensitive data? Could errors cause financial or reputational harm?
The checklist should be practical enough that a PM can complete it in 1-2 hours for a low-risk feature. If it takes a week, nobody will use it.

For bias testing, think about what a small team can realistically do: swap demographic signals in inputs, compare outputs across groups, and spot-check with diverse reviewers. You don't need a statistical framework — you need a repeatable process.
Post-launch monitoring should include both automated alerts (error rate spikes, acceptance rate drops) and periodic human reviews (monthly output quality audits)
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) Three clearly differentiated risk tiers with specific, objective classification criteria — not subjective judgments like 'feels risky.' Each tier should have 2+ concrete feature examples that demonstrate the classification logic. (2) A pre-launch checklist of 8-10 specific, actionable items (not vague categories) with risk-tier applicability marked — items should be completable and verifiable, not open-ended. (3) Five privacy questions with explanations of why each matters and what constitutes a good answer — generic questions like 'is there a privacy risk' without specifics are insufficient. (4) A bias testing protocol achievable by a small team in 2-3 days with specific test types, realistic scope, and clear decision criteria for pass/fail — protocols that require dedicated ML ethics expertise or months of work miss the practical constraint. (5) A post-launch monitoring plan with specific metrics, frequencies, and escalation triggers — 'monitor quality' without specifying what, how often, and what threshold triggers action is too vague. Deduct points for checklists that are too abstract to use in practice, privacy sections that don't address data flow through the AI API, or bias protocols that aren't feasible for a small team.

sections.title: Check Your Understanding
sections.content:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

• What's the difference between a Level 2 and Level 3 governance maturity organization, and when should a company move from one to the other?
• Why should AI governance conversations start during project planning rather than before launch?
• What's the most common privacy mistake PMs make when building AI features, and how do you avoid it?
• How would you explain the business value of bias testing to a skeptical engineering lead?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
AI governance is a PM responsibility, not just a legal or compliance function. Building governance into your AI development process protects your users, your company, and your career:

• **Governance is a product decision.** What data flows through your AI, what boundaries the system has, and how failures are handled are all product decisions with governance implications. Own them.

• **Scale governance to risk.** A low-risk internal tool needs a PM checklist. A high-risk customer-facing decision system needs a review board. Match the process to the stakes, or you'll either move too slowly or miss critical issues.

• **Start privacy conversations early.** The most common governance failure is discovering a privacy or compliance issue two weeks before launch. Begin data classification and legal review during the planning phase.

• **Bias testing is practical, not academic.** You don't need a dedicated ethics team to test for bias. Swap demographic signals, compare outputs across groups, and include diverse reviewers in your evaluation process. This can be done in days, not months.

• **Monitor after launch.** Governance doesn't end at launch. AI features can degrade, develop new failure modes, and encounter inputs that weren't anticipated. Build ongoing monitoring into your feature's operational plan.
